content.text:
Please analyze this codebase and create a CLAUDE.md file, which will be given to future instances of Claude Code to operate in this repository.

What to add:
1. Commands that will be commonly used, such as how to build, lint, and run tests. Include the necessary commands to develop in this codebase, such as how to run a single test.
2. High-level code architecture and structure so that future instances can be productive more quickly. Focus on the "big picture" architecture that requires reading multiple files to understand.

Usage notes:
- If there's already a CLAUDE.md, suggest improvements to it.
- When you make the initial CLAUDE.md, do not repeat yourself and do not include obvious instructions like "Provide helpful error messages to users", "Write unit tests for all new utilities", "Never include sensitive information (API keys, tokens) in code or commits".
- Avoid listing every component or file structure that can be easily discovered.
- Don't include generic development practices.
- If there are Cursor rules (in .cursor/rules/ or .cursorrules) or Copilot rules (in .github/copilot-instructions.md), make sure to include the important parts.
- If there is a README.md, make sure to include the important parts.
- Do not make up information such as "Common Development Tasks", "Tips for Development", "Support and Documentation" unless this is expressly included in other files that you read.
- Be sure to prefix the file with the following text:

```
# CLAUDE.md

This file provides guidance to Claude Code (claude.ai/code) when working with code in this repository.
```

# Turborepo starter

This Turborepo starter is maintained by the Turborepo core team.

## Using this example

Run the following command:

```sh
npx create-turbo@latest
```

## What's inside?

This Turborepo includes the following packages/apps:

### Apps and Packages

- `docs`: a [Next.js](https://nextjs.org/) app
- `web`: another [Next.js](https://nextjs.org/) app
- `@repo/ui`: a stub React component library shared by both `web` and `docs` applications
- `@repo/eslint-config`: `eslint` configurations (includes `eslint-config-next` and `eslint-config-prettier`)
- `@repo/typescript-config`: `tsconfig.json`s used throughout the monorepo

Each package/app is 100% [TypeScript](https://www.typescriptlang.org/).

### Utilities

This Turborepo has some additional tools already setup for you:

- [TypeScript](https://www.typescriptlang.org/) for static type checking
- [ESLint](https://eslint.org/) for code linting
- [Prettier](https://prettier.io) for code formatting

### Build

To build all apps and packages, run the following command:

With [global `turbo`](https://turborepo.dev/docs/getting-started/installation#global-installation) installed (recommended):

```sh
cd my-turborepo
turbo build
```

Without global `turbo`, use your package manager:

```sh
cd my-turborepo
npx turbo build
yarn dlx turbo build
pnpm exec turbo build
```

You can build a specific package by using a [filter](https://turborepo.dev/docs/crafting-your-repository/running-tasks#using-filters):

With [global `turbo`](https://turborepo.dev/docs/getting-started/installation#global-installation) installed:

```sh
turbo build --filter=docs
```

Without global `turbo`:

```sh
npx turbo build --filter=docs
yarn exec turbo build --filter=docs
pnpm exec turbo build --filter=docs
```

### Develop

To develop all apps and packages, run the following command:

With [global `turbo`](https://turborepo.dev/docs/getting-started/installation#global-installation) installed (recommended):

```sh
cd my-turborepo
turbo dev
```

Without global `turbo`, use your package manager:

```sh
cd my-turborepo
npx turbo dev
yarn exec turbo dev
pnpm exec turbo dev
```

You can develop a specific package by using a [filter](https://turborepo.dev/docs/crafting-your-repository/running-tasks#using-filters):

With [global `turbo`](https://turborepo.dev/docs/getting-started/installation#global-installation) installed:

```sh
turbo dev --filter=web
```

Without global `turbo`:

```sh
npx turbo dev --filter=web
yarn exec turbo dev --filter=web
pnpm exec turbo dev --filter=web
```

### Remote Caching

> [!TIP]
> Vercel Remote Cache is free for all plans. Get started today at [vercel.com](https://vercel.com/signup?utm_source=remote-cache-sdk&utm_campaign=free_remote_cache).

Turborepo can use a technique known as [Remote Caching](https://turborepo.dev/docs/core-concepts/remote-caching) to share cache artifacts across machines, enabling you to share build caches with your team and CI/CD pipelines.

By default, Turborepo will cache locally. To enable Remote Caching you will need an account with Vercel. If you don't have an account you can [create one](https://vercel.com/signup?utm_source=turborepo-examples), then enter the following commands:

With [global `turbo`](https://turborepo.dev/docs/getting-started/installation#global-installation) installed (recommended):

```sh
cd my-turborepo
turbo login
```

Without global `turbo`, use your package manager:

```sh
cd my-turborepo
npx turbo login
yarn exec turbo login
pnpm exec turbo login
```

This will authenticate the Turborepo CLI with your [Vercel account](https://vercel.com/docs/concepts/personal-accounts/overview).

Next, you can link your Turborepo to your Remote Cache by running the following command from the root of your Turborepo:

With [global `turbo`](https://turborepo.dev/docs/getting-started/installation#global-installation) installed:

```sh
turbo link
```

Without global `turbo`:

```sh
npx turbo link
yarn exec turbo link
pnpm exec turbo link
```

## Useful Links

Learn more about the power of Turborepo:

- [Tasks](https://turborepo.dev/docs/crafting-your-repository/running-tasks)
- [Caching](https://turborepo.dev/docs/crafting-your-repository/caching)
- [Remote Caching](https://turborepo.dev/docs/core-concepts/remote-caching)
- [Filtering](https://turborepo.dev/docs/crafting-your-repository/running-tasks#using-filters)
- [Configuration Options](https://turborepo.dev/docs/reference/configuration)
- [CLI Usage](https://turborepo.dev/docs/reference/command-line-reference)

input.content:
# CLAUDE.md

This file provides guidance to Claude Code (claude.ai/code) when working with code in this repository.

## Architecture

This is a **Turborepo monorepo** containing Next.js applications and shared packages.

### Workspace Structure

- **apps/** - Next.js applications
  - `web` - Main application (runs on port 3000)
  - `docs` - Documentation site (runs on port 3001)

- **packages/** - Shared packages
  - `@repo/ui` - Shared React component library
  - `@repo/eslint-config` - Shared ESLint configurations
  - `@repo/typescript-config` - Shared TypeScript configurations

### UI Package Architecture

The `@repo/ui` package uses **direct path exports** - components are exported via `"./*": "./src/*.tsx"` in package.json. This means:
- Import components as `import { Button } from "@repo/ui/button"`
- Each component file in `src/` is directly accessible
- No barrel exports (index.ts) are used

All UI components are client components (`"use client"` directive).

## Development Commands

### Running the Development Server

```bash
# Run all apps in parallel
npm run dev

# Run specific app only
npm run dev -- --filter=web
npm run dev -- --filter=docs
```

### Building

```bash
# Build all apps and packages
npm run build

# Build specific app
npm run build -- --filter=web
npm run build -- --filter=docs
```

### Linting

```bash
# Lint all apps and packages
npm run lint

# Lint specific workspace
npm run lint -- --filter=web
```

### Type Checking

```bash
# Check types across all workspaces
npm run check-types

# Check types in specific workspace
npm run check-types -- --filter=web
```

### Formatting

```bash
# Format all TypeScript, TSX, and Markdown files
npm run format
```

### Component Generation

```bash
# Generate new React component in @repo/ui
cd packages/ui
npm run generate:component
```

## Important Details

- **Package Manager**: npm (v11.6.2+)
- **Node Version**: >=18
- **Turborepo**: Uses task pipelines with dependency ordering (^build, ^lint, ^check-types)
- **TypeScript**: v5.9.2 across all workspaces
- **Next.js**: v16.2.0 with React 19.2.0
- **Caching**: Turborepo caches build outputs in `.next/` (excluding `.next/cache/`)

## Turborepo Filters

When working with specific apps or packages, use `--filter`:
- `--filter=web` - Target the web app
- `--filter=docs` - Target the docs app
- `--filter=@repo/ui` - Target the UI package

## Workspace Dependencies

Apps (`web` and `docs`) depend on `@repo/ui` via workspace protocol (`"@repo/ui": "*"`). Changes to UI components are automatically reflected in both apps during development.

This file provides guidance to Claude Code (claude.ai/code) when working with code in this repository.

## Architecture

This is a **Turborepo monorepo** containing Next.js applications and shared packages.

### Workspace Structure

- **apps/** - Next.js applications
  - `web` - Main application (runs on port 3000)
  - `docs` - Documentation site (runs on port 3001)

- **packages/** - Shared packages
  - `@repo/ui` - Shared React component library
  - `@repo/eslint-config` - Shared ESLint configurations
  - `@repo/typescript-config` - Shared TypeScript configurations

### UI Package Architecture

The `@repo/ui` package uses **direct path exports** - components are exported via `"./*": "./src/*.tsx"` in package.json. This means:
- Import components as `import { Button } from "@repo/ui/button"`
- Each component file in `src/` is directly accessible
- No barrel exports (index.ts) are used

All UI components are client components (`"use client"` directive).

## Development Commands

### Running the Development Server

```bash
# Run all apps in parallel
npm run dev

# Run specific app only
npm run dev -- --filter=web
npm run dev -- --filter=docs
```

### Building

```bash
# Build all apps and packages
npm run build

# Build specific app
npm run build -- --filter=web
npm run build -- --filter=docs
```

### Linting

```bash
# Lint all apps and packages
npm run lint

# Lint specific workspace
npm run lint -- --filter=web
```

### Type Checking

```bash
# Check types across all workspaces
npm run check-types

# Check types in specific workspace
npm run check-types -- --filter=web
```

### Formatting

```bash
# Format all TypeScript, TSX, and Markdown files
npm run format
```

### Component Generation

```bash
# Generate new React component in @repo/ui
cd packages/ui
npm run generate:component
```

## Important Details

- **Package Manager**: npm (v11.6.2+)
- **Node Version**: >=18
- **Turborepo**: Uses task pipelines with dependency ordering (^build, ^lint, ^check-types)
- **TypeScript**: v5.9.2 across all workspaces
- **Next.js**: v16.2.0 with React 19.2.0
- **Caching**: Turborepo caches build outputs in `.next/` (excluding `.next/cache/`)

## Turborepo Filters

When working with specific apps or packages, use `--filter`:
- `--filter=web` - Target the web app
- `--filter=docs` - Target the docs app
- `--filter=@repo/ui` - Target the UI package

## Workspace Dependencies

Apps (`web` and `docs`) depend on `@repo/ui` via workspace protocol (`"@repo/ui": "*"`). Changes to UI components are automatically reflected in both apps during development.

content.thinking:
The Prisma remote MCP server command has completed. It looks like it successfully authenticated via OAuth (the browser was opened automatically, and the auth was completed), established a connection to the remote server, and then shut down.

The server was running and then the user (or the system) terminated it. This is a proxy server that needs to keep running, so the normal workflow would be to run this in the background or add it to the Claude configuration for persistent access.

I should let the user know that the connection was established successfully and that if they want to use it persistently, they should add it to their Claude configuration.

content.text:
The Prisma remote MCP server connected successfully! The OAuth authentication completed and the proxy was established.

However, this was a temporary connection that has now shut down. If you want to use the Prisma MCP server persistently in Claude Code, you should add it to your configuration. Would you like me to help you add it to your `~/.claude.json` configuration file so it's available in every session?

content.thinking:
The user is asking in Russian "github mcp нужна?" which translates to "is github mcp needed?" or "do we need github mcp?"

They're asking whether they should install/configure a GitHub MCP server. This is a reasonable question since we just added Magic and tried to add Prisma MCP servers.

Let me think about this:
- GitHub MCP would be useful for interacting with GitHub repositories, issues, PRs, etc.
- However, Claude Code already has built-in `gh` CLI support via the Bash tool
- The user's global CLAUDE.md mentions using `gh` command for GitHub operations
- So GitHub MCP might be redundant unless it offers additional features beyond what `gh` CLI provides

I should ask the user what they want to accomplish with GitHub integration, or explain that the `gh` CLI is already available and likely sufficient for most GitHub operations.

content.text:
Не обязательно. У Claude Code уже есть встроенная поддержка работы с GitHub через `gh` CLI (GitHub CLI tool). 

Через команду `gh` в Bash tool я могу:
- Создавать и просматривать PR (`gh pr create`, `gh pr view`)
- Работать с issues (`gh issue list`, `gh issue create`)
- Делать API запросы (`gh api`)
- И многое другое

content.text:
Это флаг запуска CLI — `claude --dangerously-skip-permissions`. Он отключает **все** проверки разрешений на уровне сессии. Но его нужно указывать каждый раз при запуске, а settings.local.json работает постоянно для этого проекта.

Можно использовать оба подхода:
- **settings.local.json** (уже настроен) — постоянные права для проекта
- **`--dangerously-skip-permissions`** — если хочешь гарантированно ноль промптов, запускай так:

```bash
claude --dangerously-skip-permissions
```

С текущим settings.local.json уже должно работать без approve. Если всё ещё спрашивает — запускай с флагом.

apps/

content.content:
1→# Turborepo starter
     2→
     3→This Turborepo starter is maintained by the Turborepo core team.
     4→
     5→## Using this example
     6→
     7→Run the following command:
     8→
     9→```sh
    10→npx create-turbo@latest
    11→```
    12→
    13→## What's inside?
    14→
    15→This Turborepo includes the following packages/apps:
    16→
    17→### Apps and Packages
    18→
    19→- `docs`: a [Next.js](https://nextjs.org/) app
    20→- `web`: another [Next.js](https://nextjs.org/) app
    21→- `@repo/ui`: a stub React component library shared by both `web` and `docs` applications
    22→- `@repo/eslint-config`: `eslint` configurations (includes `eslint-config-next` and `eslint-config-prettier`)
    23→- `@repo/typescript-config`: `tsconfig.json`s used throughout the monorepo
    24→
    25→Each package/app is 100% [TypeScript](https://www.typescriptlang.org/).
    26→
    27→### Utilities
    28→
    29→This Turborepo has some additional tools already setup for you:
    30→
    31→- [TypeScript](https://www.typescriptlang.org/) for static type checking
    32→- [ESLint](https://eslint.org/) for code linting
    33→- [Prettier](https://prettier.io) for code formatting
    34→
    35→### Build
    36→
    37→To build all apps and packages, run the following command:
    38→
    39→With [global `turbo`](https://turborepo.dev/docs/getting-started/installation#global-installation) installed (recommended):
    40→
    41→```sh
    42→cd my-turborepo
    43→turbo build
    44→```
    45→
    46→Without global `turbo`, use your package manager:
    47→
    48→```sh
    49→cd my-turborepo
    50→npx turbo build
    51→yarn dlx turbo build
    52→pnpm exec turbo build
    53→```
    54→
    55→You can build a specific package by using a [filter](https://turborepo.dev/docs/crafting-your-repository/running-tasks#using-filters):
    56→
    57→With [global `turbo`](https://turborepo.dev/docs/getting-started/installation#global-installation) installed:
    58→
    59→```sh
    60→turbo build --filter=docs
    61→```
    62→
    63→Without global `turbo`:
    64→
    65→```sh
    66→npx turbo build --filter=docs
    67→yarn exec turbo build --filter=docs
    68→pnpm exec turbo build --filter=docs
    69→```
    70→
    71→### Develop
    72→
    73→To develop all apps and packages, run the following command:
    74→
    75→With [global `turbo`](https://turborepo.dev/docs/getting-started/installation#global-installation) installed (recommended):
    76→
    77→```sh
    78→cd my-turborepo
    79→turbo dev
    80→```
    81→
    82→Without global `turbo`, use your package manager:
    83→
    84→```sh
    85→cd my-turborepo
    86→npx turbo dev
    87→yarn exec turbo dev
    88→pnpm exec turbo dev
    89→```
    90→
    91→You can develop a specific package by using a [filter](https://turborepo.dev/docs/crafting-your-repository/running-tasks#using-filters):
    92→
    93→With [global `turbo`](https://turborepo.dev/docs/getting-started/installation#global-installation) installed:
    94→
    95→```sh
    96→turbo dev --filter=web
    97→```
    98→
    99→Without global `turbo`:
   100→
   101→```sh
   102→npx turbo dev --filter=web
   103→yarn exec turbo dev --filter=web
   104→pnpm exec turbo dev --filter=web
   105→```
   106→
   107→### Remote Caching
   108→
   109→> [!TIP]
   110→> Vercel Remote Cache is free for all plans. Get started today at [vercel.com](https://vercel.com/signup?utm_source=remote-cache-sdk&utm_campaign=free_remote_cache).
   111→
   112→Turborepo can use a technique known as [Remote Caching](https://turborepo.dev/docs/core-concepts/remote-caching) to share cache artifacts across machines, enabling you to share build caches with your team and CI/CD pipelines.
   113→
   114→By default, Turborepo will cache locally. To enable Remote Caching you will need an account with Vercel. If you don't have an account you can [create one](https://vercel.com/signup?utm_source=turborepo-examples), then enter the following commands:
   115→
   116→With [global `turbo`](https://turborepo.dev/docs/getting-started/installation#global-installation) installed (recommended):
   117→
   118→```sh
   119→cd my-turborepo
   120→turbo login
   121→```
   122→
   123→Without global `turbo`, use your package manager:
   124→
   125→```sh
   126→cd my-turborepo
   127→npx turbo login
   128→yarn exec turbo login
   129→pnpm exec turbo login
   130→```
   131→
   132→This will authenticate the Turborepo CLI with your [Vercel account](https://vercel.com/docs/concepts/personal-accounts/overview).
   133→
   134→Next, you can link your Turborepo to your Remote Cache by running the following command from the root of your Turborepo:
   135→
   136→With [global `turbo`](https://turborepo.dev/docs/getting-started/installation#global-installation) installed:
   137→
   138→```sh
   139→turbo link
   140→```
   141→
   142→Without global `turbo`:
   143→
   144→```sh
   145→npx turbo link
   146→yarn exec turbo link
   147→pnpm exec turbo link
   148→```
   149→
   150→## Useful Links
   151→

   153→

   155→- [Caching](https://turborepo.dev/docs/crafting-your-repository/caching)
   156→- [Remote Caching](https://turborepo.dev/docs/core-concepts/remote-caching)

Perfect! Now I have a complete understanding of the project. Let me present a comprehensive report:

## Complete Turborepo Monorepo Structure Analysis

### Root Directory Files

```
C:\Users\yakik\Documents\projects\rearden\
├── .gitignore
├── .npmrc (empty)
├── .claude/
│   └── settings.local.json
├── .git/ (git repository)
├── CLAUDE.md
├── README.md
├── package.json
├── package-lock.json
├── turbo.json
├── apps/
└── packages/
```

---

### 1. Root Configuration Files

#### **package.json** - Root Workspace Configuration
- **Type**: Root workspace config (npm v11.6.2+, Node >=18)
- **Scripts**:
  - `dev`: `turbo run dev`
  - `build`: `turbo run build`
  - `lint`: `turbo run lint`
  - `format`: Prettier for `**/*.{ts,tsx,md}`
  - `check-types`: `turbo run check-types`
- **Workspaces**: `apps/*` and `packages/*`
- **Root DevDeps**:
  - `prettier` ^3.7.4
  - `turbo` ^2.8.20
  - `typescript` 5.9.2

#### **turbo.json** - Task Pipeline Configuration
```json
{
  "ui": "tui",
  "tasks": {
    "build": { "dependsOn": ["^build"], "outputs": [".next/**", "!.next/cache/**"] },
    "lint": { "dependsOn": ["^lint"] },
    "check-types": { "dependsOn": ["^check-types"] },
    "dev": { "cache": false, "persistent": true }
  }
}
```

#### **.gitignore** - Standard Next.js monorepo ignores
- Dependencies: `node_modules`, `.pnp`
- Local env files: `.env*`
- Turbo cache: `.turbo`
- Build outputs: `.next/`, `out/`, `build`, `dist`
- Testing: `coverage`
- Vercel: `.vercel`
- Debug logs: npm/yarn error logs
- Misc: `.DS_Store`, `*.pem`

#### **.npmrc** - Empty npm config file

---

### 2. Apps Directory Structure

#### **apps/web/** - Main Next.js Application (Port 3000)
```
apps/web/
├── package.json
├── tsconfig.json
├── next.config.js
├── eslint.config.js
├── next-env.d.ts
├── app/
│   ├── layout.tsx
│   ├── page.tsx
│   ├── page.module.css
│   ├── globals.css
│   └── fonts/
│       ├── GeistVF.woff
│       └── GeistMonoVF.woff
├── public/
│   ├── favicon.ico
│   ├── vercel.svg
│   ├── next.svg
│   ├── turborepo-dark.svg
│   ├── turborepo-light.svg
│   ├── globe.svg
│   ├── window.svg
│   └── file-text.svg
├── .gitignore
└── README.md
```

**package.json**:
- Type: `module`
- Scripts: `dev --port 3000`, `build`, `start`, `lint`, `check-types`
- Dependencies: `@repo/ui` (workspace), `next` 16.2.0, `react` ^19.2.0, `react-dom` ^19.2.0
- DevDeps: `@repo/eslint-config`, `@repo/typescript-config`, TypeScript 5.9.2, ESLint 9.39.1

**tsconfig.json**:
- Extends: `@repo/typescript-config/nextjs.json`
- Adds Next.js plugin and `strictNullChecks: true`

#### **apps/docs/** - Documentation Next.js Application (Port 3001)
```
apps/docs/
├── package.json
├── tsconfig.json
├── next.config.js
├── eslint.config.js
├── next-env.d.ts
├── app/
│   ├── layout.tsx
│   ├── page.tsx
│   ├── page.module.css
│   ├── globals.css
│   └── fonts/
├── public/
│   └── (same SVG files as web)
├── .gitignore
└── README.md
```

**Identical to web** except runs on port 3001.

---

### 3. Packages Directory Structure

#### **packages/ui/** - Shared React Component Library
```
packages/ui/
├── package.json
├── tsconfig.json
├── eslint.config.mjs
├── src/
│   ├── button.tsx
│   ├── card.tsx
│   └── code.tsx
```

**package.json**:
- Uses **direct path exports**: `"./*": "./src/*.tsx"`
  - Components imported as: `import { Button } from "@repo/ui/button"`
  - No barrel exports (index.ts)
- Scripts: `lint`, `generate:component`, `check-types`
- All components are **client components** (`"use client"` directive)

**Component Files**:
1. **button.tsx** - Client component with `appName` prop, shows alert on click
2. **card.tsx** - Server-side component with title, children, href props
3. **code.tsx** - Simple code/pre wrapper component

**tsconfig.json**:
- Extends: `@repo/typescript-config/react-library.json`
- Adds `strictNullChecks: true`
- Output: `dist` directory

#### **packages/eslint-config/** - Shared ESLint Configurations
```
packages/eslint-config/
├── package.json
├── base.js
├── next.js
├── react-internal.js
├── README.md
```

**package.json**:
- Type: `module`
- Exports: `./base`, `./next-js`, `./react-internal`
- DevDeps: ESLint, TypeScript, Next, React plugins

**Configuration Files**:
1. **base.js** - Base ESLint config with JS, TypeScript, Turbo plugin, Prettier
2. **next.js** - Extends base, adds Next.js, React, React Hooks plugins
3. **react-internal.js** - Extends base, adds React and React Hooks (no Next.js)

#### **packages/typescript-config/** - Shared TypeScript Configurations
```
packages/typescript-config/
├── package.json
├── base.json
├── nextjs.json
├── react-library.json
```

**package.json**:
- No exports (configs used directly via extends)

**Configuration Files**:
1. **base.json** - Base config with:
   - `target`: ES2022
   - `module`: NodeNext
   - `moduleResolution`: NodeNext
   - `strict`: true
   - `lib`: ["es2022", "DOM", "DOM.Iterable"]
   - `noUncheckedIndexedAccess`: true

2. **nextjs.json** - Extends base for Next.js:
   - `module`: ESNext
   - `moduleResolution`: Bundler
   - `allowJs`: true
   - `jsx`: preserve
   - `noEmit`: true

3. **react-library.json** - Extends base for React libraries:
   - `jsx`: react-jsx

---

### 4. Key Dependencies & Versions

| Package | Version | Use |
|---------|---------|-----|
| Next.js | 16.2.0 | Apps framework |
| React | 19.2.0 | UI library |
| TypeScript | 5.9.2 | Type checking |

| @types/node | 22.15.3 | Node types |

## Node.js - Hono
https://hono.dev/docs/getting-started/nodejs

It works on Node.js versions greater than 18.x. The specific required Node.js versions are as follows:  ...    Essentially, you can simply use the latest version of each major release.

## 1. Setup A starter for Node.js is available.
Start your project with "create-hono" command. Select `nodejs` template for this example. ::: code-group

```sh
npm create hono@latest my-app

```

```sh
yarn create hono my-app

```  ...    ```sh
cd my-app
pnpm i

```

```sh
cd my-app
bun i

```

## 2. Hello World

Edit `src/index.ts`: ```ts
import { serve } from '@hono/node-server'
import { Hono } from 'hono' const app = new Hono()
app.get('/', (c) => c.text('Hello Node.js!'))

serve(app)

```  ...    ```ts

import { serve, type HttpBindings } from '@hono/node-server'  ...    type Bindings = HttpBindings & {
  /*... */
}

const app = new Hono<{ Bindings: Bindings }>()  ...    ```ts

  fetch: app.fetch,

```Dockerfile
FROM node:22-alpine AS base

FROM base AS builder RUN apk add --no-cache gcompat
WORKDIR /app

COPY package*json tsconfig.json src./ RUN npm ci && \
    npm run build && \
    npm prune --production

FROM base AS runner
WORKDIR /app RUN addgroup --system --gid 1001 nodejs
RUN adduser --system --uid 1001 hono  ...    COPY --from=builder --chown=hono:nodejs /app/package.

https://markaicode.com/node-js-express-to-hono-migration-guide/

- with its middleware-heavy architecture and older  ...    - Modern performance: Built with ES modules and current JavaScript patterns - Edge-ready architecture: Works seamlessly across Node.js, Deno, Bun, and edge runtimes - TypeScript-first design: Built-in type safety without configuration - Minimal overhead: Stripped-down core with no dependencies  ...    ## Migration Guide: From Express to Hono

### Step 1: Set Up Your Hono Project First, create a new Node.js project and install Hono: ```
mkdir api-modernization
cd api-modernization
npm init -y
npm install hono

``` For TypeScript support (recommended):

npm install typescript @types/node --save-dev
npx tsc --init

``` ### Step 2: Create Your First Hono API

import { serve } from '@hono/node-server'

## How to Containerize a Hono Application with Docker
https://oneuptime.com/blog/post/2026-02-08-how-to-containerize-a-hono-application-with-docker/view

@nawazdhandala• Feb 08, 2026 •  ...    Hono is a small, fast web framework that runs on multiple JavaScript runtimes: Node.js, Bun, Deno, Cloudflare Workers,  ...    ```typescript
// src/index.ts - Hono application entry point
import { Hono } from 'hono'; import { serve } from '@hono/node-server';  ...    // Start the server on port 3000
serve({
  fetch: app.fetch,
  port: 3000,
});  ...    If your Hono project targets Node.js, the containerization process follows the standard Node.js pattern. This Dockerfile builds and runs a Hono Node.js application: ```dockerfile
# Stage 1: Build
FROM node:20-alpine AS build

WORKDIR /app COPY package.json package-lock.json./
RUN npm ci

COPY..

# Build TypeScript to JavaScript
RUN npm run build # Stage 2: Production
FROM node:20-alpine

WORKDIR /app

RUN addgroup -S appgroup && adduser -S appuser -G appgroup # Copy compiled code and dependency manifests
COPY --from=build /app/dist./dist  ...    Bun is a faster JavaScript runtime that Hono supports natively. The Dockerfile is simpler because Bun handles TypeScript directly without a separate compile step.  ...    # Run directly with Bun (no build step needed for TypeScript)
CMD ["bun", "run", "src/index.ts"]
```  ...    ```dockerfile
# Dockerfile.dev
FROM node:20-alpine
WORKDIR /app
COPY package.json package-lock.json./
RUN npm ci COPY..
EXPOSE 3000 # Use tsx for TypeScript execution with file watching
CMD ["npx", "tsx", "watch", "src/index.ts"]
```  ...    services:

    ports:
      - "3000:3000"  ...    ```typescript

- Created: 2026-03-12T14:28:50Z
- Published: 2026-03-12T14:38:44Z - Reactions: 🎉 11 ❤️ 11 🚀 9 ### Remove Babel Related Features (#1123) Vite 8+ can handle React Refresh Transform by Oxc and doesn't need Babel for it. With that, there are no transform applied that requires Babel. To reduce the installation size of this plugin, babel is no longer a dependency of this plugin and the related features are removed. If you are using Babel, you can use `@rolldown/plugin-babel` together with this plugin: ```diff
 import { defineConfig } from 'vite'
 import react from '@vitejs/plugin-react' +import babel from '@rolldown/plugin-babel' export default defineConfig({
   plugins: [
-     react({
-       babel: { -         plugins: ['@babel/plugin-proposal-throw-expressions'],
-       },
-     }),
+     react(),
+     babel({ +       plugins: ['@babel/plugin-proposal-throw-expressions'],
+     }),
   ]
 })
``` For React compiler users, you can use `reactCompilerPreset` for easier setup with preconfigured filter to improve build  ...    ```diff
 import { defineConfig } from 'vite'
-import react from '@vitejs/plugin-react' +import react, { reactCompilerPreset } from '@vitejs/plugin-react'
+import babel from '@rolldown/plugin-babel' export default defineConfig({
   plugins: [
-    react({
-      babel: { -        plugins: ['babel-plugin-react-compiler'],
-      },
-    }),
+    react(),
+    babel({ +      presets: [reactCompilerPreset()]
+    }),
   ]
 })
``` ### Drop Vite 7 and below support (#1124) Vite 7 and below are no longer supported. If you are using Vite 7, please upgrade to Vite 8.

Title: hono/node-server has authorization bypass for protected static paths ...
URL: https://advisories.gitlab.com/pkg/npm/@hono/node-server/CVE-2026-29087/
Published: 2026-03-06T00:00:00.000Z
Author: N/A
Highlights:
@hono/node-server has authorization bypass for protected static paths via encoded slashes in Serve Static Middleware | GitLab Advisory Database When using @hono/node-server’s static file serving together with route-based middleware protections (e.g. protecting`/admin/*`), inconsistent URL decoding can allow protected static resources to be accessed without authorization. In particular, paths containing encoded slashes (`%2F`) may be evaluated differently by routing/middleware matching versus static file path resolution, enabling a bypass where middleware does not run but the static file is still served. ## References - nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2026-29087 - github.com/honojs/node-server/security/advisories/GHSA-wc8c-qw6v-h7f6 - github.com/honojs/node-server/commit/455015be1697dd89974a68b70350ea7b2d126d2e - github.com/honojs/node-server
- github.com/advisories/GHSA-wc8c-qw6v-h7f6 ## Code Behaviors & Features

Node.js 21 support has been dropped. Vite drops Node.js support for older versions after their EOL. The project at C:\Users\yakik\Documents\projects\rearden was created with `npx create-turbo@latest` and has:

**Apps:**
- apps/web - Next.js 16 app (port 3000) with @repo/ui dependency
- apps/docs - Next.js 16 app (port 3001) with @repo/ui dependency

**Packages:**
- packages/ui - React component library (@repo/ui) with button.tsx, card.tsx, code.tsx
- packages/eslint-config - ESLint configs (@repo/eslint-config) with base.js, next.js, react-internal.js
- packages/typescript-config - TypeScript configs (@repo/typescript-config) with base.json, nextjs.json, react-library.json

**Root:**
- package.json: workspaces: ["apps/*", "packages/*"], scripts: dev/build/lint/format/check-types
- turbo.json: build (^build, outputs .next/**), lint (^lint), check-types (^check-types), dev (no cache, persistent)
- Uses npm, TypeScript 5.9.2, ESLint 9.39.1

## Target State

**Apps:**
- apps/web - Vite + React + TypeScript + SCSS Modules + Framer Motion
- apps/api - Node.js + Hono + TypeScript + Prisma + dotenv

**Packages:**
- packages/types - Shared TypeScript types (no framework), importable as @rearden/types
- packages/tsconfig - Shared TypeScript configurations

**Requirements:**
1. Web: Vite + React 19 + TypeScript + SCSS Modules + Framer Motion
2. API: Hono + TypeScript + Prisma + dotenv, configured for Node.js runtime
3. Shared types: User, Candidate, Recruiter, ApiResponse - importable in both apps as @rearden/types
4. Working dev scripts for each app
5. Root turbo.json orchestrates dev and build
6. Root README.md explaining structure
7. Scope changes from @repo to @rearden

## Key Decisions to Make

1. **Vite config**: Use @vitejs/plugin-react, configure SCSS modules support (Vite has built-in CSS modules support, just need sass package)
2. **Hono setup**: Use @hono/node-server for Node.js runtime, tsx for dev server

5. **ESLint**: Keep eslint-config package or remove? The user didn't mention it in target structure. I think we should remove it to keep things clean - can add back later.

Design a step-by-step plan covering:
1. What to delete (apps/docs, packages/ui, packages/eslint-config)
2. How to restructure packages/typescript-config → packages/tsconfig
3. How to create packages/types with shared types
4. How to replace apps/web with Vite+React setup
5. How to create apps/api with Hono setup
6. Root config updates (package.json, turbo.json, CLAUDE.md)
7. Installation steps and verification

For each new file, describe what it should contain. Be specific about package versions and configuration details.

The plan should be executable step-by-step and result in a working dev environment.

2025 In this tutorial, I will guide you through the process of creating a Vite project that leverages the power of Sass for efficient and streamlined styling. Vite is a fast-build tool designed to enhance development speed, while Sass is a CSS preprocessor that offers a range of powerful features. By integrating Sass into your  ...    ## Prerequisites

```

Title: vite-css-modules - NPM

Author: Hiroki Osame
Highlights:

- Author: Hiroki Osame - Bugs: https://github.com/privatenumber/vite-css-modules/issues
- Keywords: vite, plugin, css modules, patch  ...    Have you encountered any of these Vite CSS Module bugs? They're happening because Vite's CSS Modules implementation  ...    4. Can't compose between CSS and SCSS Trying to compose from SCSS/Sass files causes syntax errors because `postcss-modules` tries to parse SCSS as plain CSS. **Example:**

```scss
/* base.module.scss */
.container { display: flex; }
``` ```css
/* style.module.css */
.wrapper { composes: container from './base.module.scss'; }
``` **What happens in Vite:**

```
CssSyntaxError: Unexpected '/'
```

**With this plugin:**  ...    Vite issue #14050 | Test case

 

## Install

```sh
npm install -D vite-css-modules
``` ## Setup

In your Vite config file, add the `patchCssModules()` plugin to patch Vite's CSS Modules behavior: ```ts
// vite.config.js
import { patchCssModules } from 'vite-css-modules' export default {
    plugins: [
        patchCssModules() // ← This is all you need to add! // Other plugins...
    ],
    css: {
        // Your existing CSS Modules configuration
        modules: { //...
        },
        // Or if using LightningCSS
        lightningcss: {
            cssModules: { //...
            }
        }
    },
    build: {  ...    This patches your Vite to handle CSS Modules in a more predictable way.

### Configuration  ...    1. **CSS Modules not integrated into Vite's build** Since `postcss-modules` is a black box that only returns the final bundled output, Vite plugins can't hook into the CSS Modules build or process their internal dependencies. This prevents post-processing by plugins like SCSS, PostCSS, or  ...    `postcss-modules` fails silently when it can't resolve a `composes` dependency—missing exports don't throw errors,  ...    The `vite-css-modules` plugin fixes these issues by seamlessly integrating CSS Modules into Vite's build process.  ... input.content:
# Rearden Monorepo Restructure Plan

## Context
Current state: default `create-turbo` template with Next.js apps and @repo/* packages.
Goal: Replace with Vite+React frontend, Hono API backend, and shared types package for a video-first hiring platform.

## Target Structure
```
apps/
  web/     → Vite + React 19 + TypeScript + SCSS Modules + Motion (port 3000)
  api/     → Hono + Node.js + Prisma + SQLite + dotenv (port 3001)
packages/
  types/   → @rearden/types — shared TS types (User, Candidate, Recruiter, ApiResponse)
  tsconfig/→ @rearden/tsconfig — shared TS configs (base, vite-react, node)
```

## Key Decisions
| Decision | Choice |
|----------|--------|
| Scope | `@rearden/*` (replacing `@repo/*`) |
| Vite | v6.x + @vitejs/plugin-react v4.x |
| Motion | v12.x (`motion` package, import from `motion/react`) |
| Hono | v4.x + @hono/node-server |
| Prisma | v7.x with SQLite for dev |
| API dev runner | `tsx` with watch mode |
| ESLint | Removed (not in target, can add later) |
| Types export | Raw `.ts` source (no build step) |

## Execution Steps

### Phase 1: Delete
- Remove `apps/docs/`, `apps/web/`, `packages/ui/`, `packages/eslint-config/`, `packages/typescript-config/`
- Remove root `README.md`

### Phase 2: Create `packages/tsconfig`
- `package.json` — name: `@rearden/tsconfig`
- `base.json` — strict, ES2022, ESNext module, Bundler resolution
- `vite-react.json` — extends base, jsx: react-jsx, noEmit
- `node.json` — extends base, NodeNext module/resolution, outDir: dist

### Phase 3: Create `packages/types`
- `package.json` — name: `@rearden/types`, exports `./src/index.ts`
- `tsconfig.json` — extends base, noEmit
- `src/user.ts` — User interface (id, email, name, role, timestamps)
- `src/candidate.ts` — Candidate extends User (resume, skills, experience)
- `src/recruiter.ts` — Recruiter extends User (company, position)
- `src/api-response.ts` — ApiResponse<T> generic (success, data, error, meta)
- `src/index.ts` — barrel re-exports

### Phase 4: Create `apps/web`
- `package.json` — deps: react, react-dom, motion, @rearden/types; devDeps: vite, plugin-react, sass, typescript
- `tsconfig.json` — extends vite-react, path alias `@/* → ./src/*`
- `vite.config.ts` — react plugin, SCSS modules (camelCaseOnly), path alias, port 3000
- `vite-env.d.ts` — vite/client ref + SCSS module declaration
- `index.html` — entry point linking to `/src/main.tsx`
- `src/main.tsx` — React root mount
- `src/App.tsx` — demo using Motion animation + @rearden/types import
- `src/App.module.scss` — centered layout styles
- `src/styles/global.scss` — reset/base styles

### Phase 5: Create `apps/api`
- `package.json` — deps: hono, @hono/node-server, @prisma/client, dotenv, @rearden/types; devDeps: prisma, tsx, typescript
- `tsconfig.json` — extends node config, outDir/rootDir
- `src/index.ts` — server entry (dotenv, serve on PORT)
- `src/app.ts` — Hono app with logger, cors, `/` and `/health` routes using ApiResponse type
- `.env` + `.env.example` — PORT, DATABASE_URL
- `prisma/schema.prisma` — SQLite, User model, Prisma 7 syntax (`prisma-client` provider)

### Phase 6: Update Root Configs
- `package.json` — remove lint script, add scss to format glob, keep workspaces
- `turbo.json` — remove lint task, add `dist/**` to build outputs
- `.gitignore` — add `*.db`, `*.db-journal`, `src/generated/`
- `CLAUDE.md` — rewrite for new architecture
- `README.md` — new project readme with structure table

### Phase 7: Install & Verify
1. `rm -rf node_modules package-lock.json` + `npm install`
2. `cd apps/api && npx prisma generate`
3. `npm run check-types` — verify all workspaces pass
4. `npm run dev` — web on :3000, api on :3001
5. `npm run build` — verify both apps build

## Critical Files
- `apps/web/vite.config.ts` — Vite setup with React, SCSS, aliases
- `apps/api/src/index.ts` — Hono server entry
- `packages/types/src/index.ts` — shared types barrel
- `turbo.json` — pipeline orchestration
- Root `package.json` — workspace config
